days.title: Day 1: Asking for things — дай / дайте

grammar.title: The imperative: дай vs. дайте
grammar.explanation: To ask someone to **give** you something, use the imperative. **дай** is the informal singular form (to a friend or family). **дайте** is the polite or plural form (to a stranger, an older person, or several people) — it simply adds **-те**. Almost always pair it with **пожалуйста** to sound polite. For now, just memorise the request phrases as fixed expressions (e.g. **Дайте меню**, **Дайте чай**, **Дайте воду**).

reading.title: В кафе
reading.text: — Дайте меню, пожалуйста. — Вот меню. — Спасибо. И дайте воду.
reading.translation: — Give me the menu, please. — Here's the menu. — Thank you. And give me water.

days.speakingPrompt: Imagine you are in a cafe. Out loud, politely ask the waiter for the menu, then for water, then for the bill — using дайте and пожалуйста each time.

days.title: Day 2: Speak and tell — скажи / скажите

vocab.exampleEn: Tell me where the bank is.
Tell me, where is the metro?
Excuse me, where is the metro?
Tell me, where is the pharmacy?
Excuse me, where is the bank?

grammar.title: Asking for information: скажите
grammar.explanation: **скажите** (tell / say, polite) is the standard way to start a question to a stranger. Like **дайте**, the polite form ends in **-те**; the informal form is **скажи**. A very natural opener is **Извините, скажите, ...** (Excuse me, tell me, ...). Follow it with a **где**-question to ask where something is.

examples.english: Tell me, where is the metro?
Excuse me, tell me, where is the bank?
Tell me, where is the pharmacy? (informal)

reading.title: На улице
reading.text: — Извините, скажите, где метро? — Метро здесь, налево. — Спасибо!
reading.translation: — Excuse me, tell me, where is the metro? — The metro is here, to the left. — Thank you!

days.speakingPrompt: You are lost in a Russian city. Stop a stranger politely and ask where the metro, the pharmacy, and the bank are, starting each question with Извините, скажите.

days.title: Day 3: Help me — помоги / помогите

grammar.title: Asking for help and clarification
grammar.explanation: More useful polite imperatives all end in **-те**: **помогите** (help), **повторите** (repeat), **говорите** (speak). As a learner, two phrases are gold: **Повторите, пожалуйста** (Please repeat) and **Говорите медленно, пожалуйста** (Please speak slowly). Add **пожалуйста** to soften any command.

reading.title: Урок
reading.text: — Извините, говорите медленно, пожалуйста. — Конечно. Повторите вопрос. — Спасибо, помогите.
reading.translation: — Excuse me, please speak slowly. — Of course. Repeat the question. — Thank you, help me.

days.speakingPrompt: Practice survival phrases for a conversation you don't fully understand: say out loud Повторите, пожалуйста and Говорите медленно, пожалуйста, then ask for help with Помогите, пожалуйста.

days.title: Day 4: May I? — можно

vocab.english: may / is it allowed / one can

grammar.title: Polite requests with можно
grammar.explanation: **можно** means *may I / is it allowed?* It is one of the most useful request words. Say **Можно** + a noun to ask for something: **Можно меню?** (May I have the menu?). Its opposite is **нельзя** (not allowed). **можно** alone with rising intonation is a complete, polite question.

reading.title: В музее
reading.text: — Извините, можно сюда? — Нет, здесь нельзя. Выход там. — Спасибо.
reading.translation: — Excuse me, may I go this way? — No, it's not allowed here. The exit is over there. — Thank you.

days.speakingPrompt: Use можно to ask permission: out loud, ask Можно меню?, Можно воду?, and Можно сюда? Then practice understanding нельзя as the answer no.

days.title: Day 5: Polite extras — будьте добры

vocab.english: would you be so kind / please
vocab.exampleEn: Would you be so kind, the bill.

grammar.title: Softer requests and hosting phrases
grammar.explanation: To sound extra polite, start a request with **Будьте добры, ...** (Would you be so kind). When you host or receive someone, use the welcoming imperatives **садитесь** (have a seat), **проходите** (come in), and **подождите** (please wait). Respond to thanks with **Не за что** (You're welcome). These are all set polite forms ending in **-те** / **-тесь**.

examples.english: Would you be so kind, the bill.
Come in, have a seat, please.
Thank you very much! — You're welcome.

reading.title: Гость
reading.text: — Здравствуйте! Проходите, садитесь, пожалуйста. — Спасибо большое. — Не за что.
reading.translation: — Hello! Come in, have a seat, please. — Thank you very much. — You're welcome.

days.speakingPrompt: Role-play welcoming a guest to your home: say Проходите, садитесь, пожалуйста. Then practice asking a waiter politely with Будьте добры, чек and replying to thanks with Не за что.

days.title: Day 6: Putting requests together

grammar.title: Combining commands and polite words
grammar.explanation: Now mix everything: an opener (**Извините** / **Будьте добры**), an imperative (**дайте**, **покажите**, **напишите**, **повторите**), and **пожалуйста**. More polite imperatives: **покажите** (show), **напишите** (write). Useful add-ons: **ещё раз** (again), **помедленнее** (a bit slower). Word order is flexible, but **пожалуйста** usually sits at the end or right after the verb.

reading.title: В ресторане
reading.text: — Официант, будьте добры, покажите меню. — Минуточку. Вот меню. — Спасибо. И дайте счёт, пожалуйста.
reading.translation: — Waiter, would you be so kind, show me the menu. — Just a moment. Here's the menu. — Thank you. And give me the check, please.

days.speakingPrompt: Build full polite requests out loud: ask a waiter to show the menu (Покажите меню, пожалуйста), ask them to write the address down (Напишите, пожалуйста), and ask for the check (Дайте счёт, пожалуйста).

days.title: Day 7: Review — requests & commands

grammar.title: Recap: imperatives and polite requests
grammar.explanation: This week you learned to make requests. **Imperatives** come in two forms: informal (**дай, скажи, помоги**) and polite/plural with **-те** (**дайте, скажите, помогите, повторите, говорите, покажите, напишите, подождите, садитесь, проходите**). For permission, use **можно** (may I) and its opposite **нельзя** (not allowed). Politeness markers: **пожалуйста**, **извините**, **будьте добры**, and the reply **не за что**. Soften any command by adding **пожалуйста**.

examples.english: Tell me, where is the metro?

reading.title: Один день
reading.text: — Извините, скажите, где аптека? — Там, налево. — Спасибо большое! — Не за что. — И помогите, пожалуйста: говорите медленно. — Конечно.
reading.translation: — Excuse me, tell me, where is the pharmacy? — Over there, to the left. — Thank you very much! — You're welcome. — And please help me: speak slowly. — Of course.

days.speakingPrompt: Combine the whole week into one short scene: greet someone, ask where the pharmacy is (Скажите, где аптека?), thank them (Спасибо большое), ask them to speak slowly (Говорите медленно, пожалуйста), and order water in a cafe with Можно воду? Try to use дайте, скажите, помогите, можно, and пожалуйста at least once each.
